lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Twenty-two minutes past nine on a Tuesday in February, and St. Brendan's Emergency Department has run out of corridor space. Trolleys line the east wall in a row that bends around the nursing station. Every monitor beeps at a different interval. The overhead fluorescents hum their one note without mercy.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Maeve Callahan stands at the triage desk with a chart in each hand and a third tucked under her arm. She has not sat down since half six.

lines.speaker: Maeve
lines.text: Reza — the chest pain in bay two, what's his pressure doing?

lines.speaker: Reza
lines.text: Still one-ten over seventy. He keeps asking for water.

lines.speaker: Maeve
lines.text: Nothing by mouth until cardio clears him. Tell him I'll be there in five minutes and mean it.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: She does not get there in five minutes. A woman in her seventies arrives mid-sentence, arm crooked at an angle that tells Maeve everything before any words are exchanged. Behind her, a teenager with a split scalp, still wearing a football jersey. Behind him, a man holding his ribs with both hands, breathing in short, careful sips.

lines.speaker: Maeve
lines.text: Theo, I need you now. Three incoming, possible fracture, laceration needing closure, query pneumothorax — take the rib guy first.

lines.speaker: Theo
lines.text: Bay six is still occupied. Waiting on a porter.

lines.speaker: Maeve
lines.text: Then use the assessment room. Move.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Theo moves. He is good at moving when she uses that voice. He has learned, in the six months since she arrived at St. Brendan's, that there is a register she shifts into when the building starts to tilt — clipped, exact, carrying no weight she does not need to carry. He finds it easier to obey than to wonder about it.

lines.speaker: Reza
lines.text: Maeve. Ward three just rang. They're sending two discharges down but the beds aren't prepped yet — porters are all tied up.

lines.speaker: Maeve
lines.text: Then they wait in ward three. We cannot generate space we don't have.

lines.speaker: Reza
lines.text: They're saying management wants the throughput numbers by midnight.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: She sets one chart on the desk and picks up the phone. She holds it for two seconds without dialling, then puts it back down.

lines.speaker: Maeve
lines.text: Tell them I'm aware of the time.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: The chest pain man calls out from bay two. Reza goes. The teenager with the laceration has sat himself on a vacant chair and is pressing his sleeve against his scalp with the casual patience of someone who has done this before. Maeve pauses at him on her way past.

lines.speaker: Maeve
lines.text: Pressure on, not tight. Keep your elbow up. I'll have someone with you in ten.

lines.speaker: Teenager
lines.text: Am I going to need stitches?

lines.speaker: Maeve
lines.text: Probably. You'll live.

lines.speaker: Teenager
lines.text: That's what my da always says.

lines.speaker: Maeve
lines.text: Smart man.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: She is already moving again. Through the narrow gap between trolleys. Past the family who arrived an hour ago and have stopped asking questions because they can see what the room is. Past the whiteboard where the column for available bays shows three slots, all of them qualified with asterisks that mean conditional, pending, not quite.

lines.speaker: Theo
lines.text: Maeve. Rib guy — I think we're looking at a pneumo. Breath sounds are reduced on the left.

lines.speaker: Maeve
lines.text: Get me a chest film and ring respiratory. If he deteriorates before they answer, come back to me immediately.

lines.speaker: Theo
lines.text: Got it.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: Theo disappears. Reza reappears. The whiteboard gains another name in the waiting column. Somewhere behind the curtain in bay five, a monitor alarm begins to sound and then stops, which is either better or worse, and Maeve has already turned toward it before she knows she has decided to move.

lines.speaker: NARRATOR
lines.text: This is the shape of the night. Not chaos — chaos would be simpler. This is a system running past its tolerances with people inside it doing their best to hold the shape of normal. Maeve has held this shape before, in other buildings, other corridors. She knows exactly how much further it can bend. She does not know yet that in eleven minutes someone will close two of the bays that remain.
